summary: This advice column addresses reader questions on birth control options, finding authentic adult content, and why men and women report different numbers of sexual partners. The central discussion explores the statistical paradox that average partner counts must be equal for heterosexual men and women, yet surveys consistently show discrepancies. O'Neil examines research showing people lie to match gender expectations, with studies using lie detectors revealing more honest responses. She also touches on how differing definitions of 'sex' (whether oral counts) contribute to reporting gaps, noting this may reflect gendered power dynamics in sexual encounters.
key_points: The average number of sexual partners for straight men and women must mathematically be equal - any survey discrepancy means someone is lying
Research shows men inflate and women deflate their reported partner counts to match social expectations about masculinity and femininity
When hooked to lie detectors, the gender gap in reported partners disappears or even reverses
Differing definitions of what counts as 'sex' contribute to discrepancies, with oral sex often only benefiting men and thus not being counted by women
The column playfully combines data science rigor with frank sex advice, treating both as equally worthy of analysis
money_quotes: Let's just agree, once and for all, that if you actually got a good sample of all (straight) men and all (straight) women... then the average number of sexual partners would have to be equal. It's just a statistical truth.
We can't explain the discrepancy in any way except that woman and/or men are lying about the number of people they've slept with.
When 'oral sex' occurs, it's often only the guy getting it! And then in what sense has the woman really had sex?
Kind of annoying that the most obvious choices are so hard on a woman's body, isn't it?
One of the reasons I'm proud to call myself a data scientist.
key_insight: Simple arithmetic proves that reported gender differences in sexual partner counts must reflect social pressure and dishonesty rather than reality, demonstrating how even basic statistics can expose cultural biases.
